description: The design and regulation of land use, transportation, community facilities, and infrastructure. How places are shaped by intention and neglect.

dome_relevance.5: Housing — zoning, density, affordability as planning outcomes
dome_relevance.8: Community — neighborhood design shapes social connection
dome_relevance.9: Environment — land use determines environmental exposure

sphere_relevance.1: Parcel — zoning IS the planning framework
sphere_relevance.2: Infrastructure — utilities, transit, connectivity as planned systems
sphere_relevance.4: Economic — land value, TIF districts, development finance
sphere_relevance.5: Social — demographics and foot traffic shaped by planning decisions
sphere_relevance.7: Activation — what a space becomes is a planning question

adjacent_disciplines.description: Design of outdoor spaces, ecological systems, terrain

practitioners.name: Jane Jacobs
practitioners.practice: Urban observation, community-scale economics, anti-demolition activism
practitioners.approach: Eyes on the street. The city is not a problem to be solved by experts — it's a living system understood by watching how people actually use it. Diversity of use, short blocks, old buildings, density of people. These are not disorder — they are the immune system of a neighborhood.
practitioners.body_of_work: The Death and Life of Great American Cities (1961), The Economy of Cities (1969), Systems of Survival (1992). Stopped the Lower Manhattan Expressway. Fought Robert Moses. Proved that small-scale, mixed-use, high-density neighborhoods outperform planned superblocks.
practitioners.why_instructive: She saw what the sphere schema sees — a place is a living system with layers. Her fight against Moses is the fight against treating places as problems rather than organisms.

dome_relevance.5: Housing as community fabric, not units
dome_relevance.8: Community as emergent property of good design

sphere_relevance.1: Zoning critique — mixed use over segregated use
sphere_relevance.4: Economic vitality from diversity, not monoculture
sphere_relevance.5: Social fabric emerges from physical design
sphere_relevance.6: Temporal depth — old buildings subsidize new uses
sphere_relevance.7: Activation as natural byproduct of good urban form
sphere_relevance.10: Catalyst — one good block activates the next

practitioners.name: Robert Moses
practitioners.practice: Mega-scale infrastructure, highway construction, urban renewal, power accumulation
practitioners.approach: The master builder: reshape the city from above. Highways through neighborhoods. Parks as monuments. Public housing as towers. The person is irrelevant — the system is everything. Speed, scale, throughput.
practitioners.body_of_work: Built virtually every major highway, bridge, tunnel, park, and public housing project in New York from 1924-1968. Displaced hundreds of thousands. Created Jones Beach, the Cross-Bronx Expressway, Lincoln Center. Robert Caro's The Power Broker documents the full scope.
practitioners.why_instructive: He's the anti-pattern. He shows what happens when infrastructure serves systems instead of people. Every dome layer he touched, he damaged. His work is instructive as a cautionary tale — the dome must never replicate his logic.

dome_relevance.1: Used legal mechanisms (eminent domain, authorities) to override community rights
dome_relevance.2: Built systems that served cars, not people
dome_relevance.8: Demolished communities — the Cross-Bronx Expressway as community destruction

sphere_relevance.1: Parcel seizure through eminent domain at industrial scale
sphere_relevance.2: Infrastructure as violence — highways severing neighborhoods
sphere_relevance.5: Social fabric torn by forced relocation

practitioners.name: Howard Roark
practitioners.creator: Ayn Rand
practitioners.practice: Architecture as individual vision, uncompromising design integrity
practitioners.approach: The building is the architect's vision, unmediated by committee, compromise, or community input. Form follows the creator's will. Buildings are not for occupants — they are expressions of genius.
practitioners.body_of_work: Designs buildings that embody his singular vision. Destroys Cortlandt Homes when his design is altered. Refuses commissions that require compromise. The novel presents this as heroic.
practitioners.why_instructive: He's another anti-pattern — the architect who doesn't listen. Sphere activation requires the opposite of Roark: design that emerges from community, not genius imposed upon it. His perspective is instructive by inversion.

dome_relevance.11: Creativity as individual expression — the question of whose creativity matters

sphere_relevance.7: Activation designed without community input — what goes wrong
sphere_relevance.8: Permanence designed for the architect, not the community

practitioners.name: Hideo Kojima's portrayal of cities in Policenauts
practitioners.creator: Hideo Kojima
practitioners.practice: Space colony urban design as a mirror to Earth's cities
practitioners.approach: A colony in space replicates every urban problem: crime, corruption, pharmaceutical exploitation, surveillance. The physical infrastructure is perfect. The social infrastructure fails in identical ways. Technology changes the container but not the content.
practitioners.body_of_work: Beyond Colony — a space station that reproduces Los Angeles's social pathology in a designed environment. Shows that planning perfect infrastructure doesn't solve human problems.
practitioners.why_instructive: Demonstrates that sphere layers 1-6 (the AI-fillable infrastructure layers) are necessary but not sufficient. Without layers 7-10 (human design), a technically perfect place still fails.

dome_relevance.2: Systems that work technically but fail socially
dome_relevance.5: Housing that is adequate by metrics but alienating by experience

sphere_relevance.2: Perfect infrastructure doesn't equal good place
sphere_relevance.5: Social layer reflects power structures regardless of physical design
sphere_relevance.7: The absence of meaningful activation in a 'perfect' environment

practitioners.name: Octavia Butler's Lauren Olamina
practitioners.creator: Octavia Butler
practitioners.practice: Community building in collapse, hyperempathy as design constraint, founding Earthseed
practitioners.approach: When existing communities collapse, build new ones from whoever survives. Hyperempathy — literally feeling others' pain — as the foundation for community design. The only lasting truth is Change. Shape it or be destroyed by it.
practitioners.body_of_work: Lauren Olamina navigates a collapsed California (2024-2027), building Acorn community from refugees, ex-slaves, and wanderers. Earthseed's principles: God is Change, shape Change or be shaped. Creates community through radical inclusion under shared purpose.
practitioners.why_instructive: She builds a dome from scratch — not through systems but through shared philosophy. Her hyperempathy is the design constraint: you CANNOT ignore suffering because you literally feel it. This is what person-centered means.

dome_relevance.4: Hyperempathy as health condition that shapes all interactions
dome_relevance.10: Autonomy — choosing to lead, choosing to build
dome_relevance.12: Flourishing defined as purposeful adaptation to change

sphere_relevance.5: Social fabric woven from strangers through shared purpose
sphere_relevance.6: Temporal — building from the ruins of what was
sphere_relevance.7: Activation — Acorn as intentional community activation
sphere_relevance.8: Permanence — what lasts when everything else collapses
